oxford-balliol.name: Balliol College
oxford-balliol.overview: One of Oxford's oldest and most academically distinguished colleges, Balliol was founded around 1263 by John I de Balliol and formally established by his widow Dervorguilla of Galloway in 1282. The college occupies a prime central position on Broad Street and has a student body of approximately 399 undergraduates and 302 postgraduates.
oxford-balliol.character: Known for its left-leaning intellectual culture, meritocratic ethos, and strong PPE tradition. Balliol has a reputation for producing prime ministers and public intellectuals, and prizes academic ambition alongside political engagement.

accommodation.description: First and third years live on the main Broad Street site; second years can opt for private accommodation or college-owned flats at Jowett Walk or Master's Field, a short walk away. Postgraduates are housed at Holywell Manor.

keyFacts: One of Oxford's three oldest colleges, founded c.1263
Has produced 4 British Prime Ministers
13 Nobel Prize winners among alumni
Strong tradition of PPE and political engagement
Located on Broad Street in the heart of Oxford

finances.bursaries: Oxford Bursary available for students from low-income households; Balliol also offers additional college support
finances.description: A well-endowed college with strong alumni giving and a commitment to access through bursaries and outreach.

oxford-brasenose.name: Brasenose College
oxford-brasenose.overview: Brasenose College was founded in 1509 by William Smyth, Bishop of Lincoln, and Sir Richard Sutton, the first lay founder of an Oxford college. Its name derives from a brazen (bronze) door knocker. The college has 382 undergraduates and 201 postgraduates.
oxford-brasenose.character: Known for a strong sporting culture alongside genuine academic excellence, Brasenose has a reputation as one of Oxford's more sociable and well-rounded colleges. It placed second in the Norrington Table in 2023.

accommodation.description: The college provides accommodation for all three years of an undergraduate's course, with rooms on the main site near Radcliffe Square as well as in nearby annexes.

keyFacts: Founded 1509 by Bishop Smyth and Sir Richard Sutton
Named after a historic bronze door knocker
Produced 2 British and 2 Australian prime ministers
3 Nobel Prize winners among alumni
Second in the Norrington Table in 2023

finances.bursaries: Oxford Bursary scheme plus college-specific support
finances.description: A mid-tier endowment with strong alumni engagement and commitment to student support.

oxford-christ-church.name: Christ Church
oxford-christ-church.overview: Christ Church is Oxford's largest and most architecturally spectacular college, founded in 1546 by King Henry VIII as a unique joint foundation of the university and the cathedral of the Oxford diocese. The college has 451 undergraduates and 172 postgraduates, and its chapel doubles as Oxford Cathedral.
oxford-christ-church.character: Historically associated with wealth, grandeur, and the English establishment, Christ Church has a reputation for social prestige alongside genuine academic distinction. It has produced more British prime ministers than any other Oxford college.

accommodation.description: Accommodation is provided for all years, with rooms in the iconic main site including the famous Tom Quad, as well as in college-owned properties nearby. The college's extensive grounds include Christ Church Meadow.

keyFacts: Oxford's largest college by student numbers
Founded 1546 by Henry VIII
Produced 13 British Prime Ministers
College chapel is Oxford Cathedral
Famous alumni include Lewis Carroll, John Locke, and W. H. Auden

finances.bursaries: Generous Oxford Bursary and college-specific hardship funds
finances.description: The wealthiest Oxford college, with an endowment exceeding £577 million, enabling extensive student support.

oxford-corpus-christi.name: Corpus Christi College
oxford-corpus-christi.overview: Corpus Christi College was founded in 1517 by Richard Fox, Bishop of Winchester, as a humanist institution emphasising Greek and Latin scholarship. It is one of Oxford's smaller colleges, with around 235 undergraduates and approximately 120 postgraduates, set around a picturesque quadrangle.
oxford-corpus-christi.character: A small, intimate, and academically high-performing college with a strong humanities tradition. Corpus is known for its friendly community, beautiful medieval buildings, and its famous pelican sundial in the main quad.

accommodation.description: The college guarantees accommodation for all undergraduate years, mostly within or immediately adjacent to the main site near Merton Street.

notableAlumni: John Rainolds (Originator of King James Bible)
keyFacts: Founded 1517 by Bishop Richard Fox
One of Oxford's smallest undergraduate colleges
Famous for its pelican sundial in the main quad
Strong humanities tradition since its humanist foundation
Endowment of ~£58m

finances.bursaries: Oxford Bursary plus college support funds
finances.description: A smaller endowment but strong commitment to student welfare and access.

oxford-exeter.name: Exeter College
oxford-exeter.overview: Exeter College is the fourth-oldest college at Oxford, founded in 1314 by Walter de Stapeldon, Bishop of Exeter, on Turl Street where it has remained ever since. The college has 344 undergraduates and 253 postgraduates, and is known for its award-winning Cohen Quad development in Jericho.
oxford-exeter.character: A mid-sized, internationally minded college with a warm and friendly atmosphere. Exeter is known for producing prominent figures in the arts and literature, and for having one of the most architecturally striking modern additions (Cohen Quad) to any Oxford college.

accommodation.description: Accommodation is offered for all three years. First years live on the main Turl Street site; later years have access to the Cohen Quad in Jericho and other college properties in East Oxford with self-catering facilities.

keyFacts: Fourth-oldest Oxford college, founded 1314
Located on Turl Street in central Oxford
Home to the award-winning modern Cohen Quad
Strong literary alumni including Tolkien and Pullman
International atmosphere with students from around the world

finances.bursaries: Oxford Bursary and college-specific financial support
finances.description: Well-resourced college with significant recent capital investment in new accommodation and facilities.

oxford-harris-manchester.name: Harris Manchester College
oxford-harris-manchester.overview: Harris Manchester College is Oxford's only college exclusively for mature students (aged 21 and over), with roots going back to 1757 as the Warrington Academy for Nonconformist students. It moved to Oxford in 1893 and achieved full college status in 1996. It is the smallest undergraduate college in either Oxford or Cambridge, with around 100 undergraduates and 150 postgraduates.
oxford-harris-manchester.character: An intimate, welcoming, and distinctly non-traditional college with a strong commitment to access and mature student education. The community is diverse in age and background, creating a collegial atmosphere quite different from standard Oxford undergraduate colleges.

accommodation.description: All accommodation is on the main college site. Students can generally live in college for at least the first and final years of their course.

notableAlumni: Lord Harris of Peckham (Benefactor and Businessman)
keyFacts: Oxford's only college exclusively for mature students (21+)
Founded 1757 as the Warrington Academy
Smallest undergraduate college at Oxford or Cambridge
Achieved full college status in 1996
Strong Nonconformist and liberal educational heritage

finances.bursaries: Oxford Bursary and college hardship funds available
finances.description: Smaller endowment but strong donor support following the naming gift from Lord Harris of Peckham.

oxford-hertford.name: Hertford College
oxford-hertford.overview: Hertford College has a complex history tracing back to Hart Hall in the 1280s, but was refounded in its modern form by Act of Parliament in 1874. Located on Catte Street next to the Bodleian Library, the college has about 400 undergraduates and 200 postgraduates, and is famous for its 'Bridge of Sighs' linking two of its buildings.
oxford-hertford.character: One of Oxford's more progressive colleges, Hertford was a pioneer of state school access from the 1960s and has a higher-than-average proportion of state-educated students. It has a friendly, informal atmosphere and a strong access record.

accommodation.description: Undergraduates typically reside in college-owned accommodation for all three years, with rooms cleaned daily. Three meals are available each weekday in the college hall. Accommodation is spread across the main site and several annexes near Folly Bridge.

notableAlumni: William Tyndale (Bible Translator, via Hart Hall)
keyFacts: Modern college founded by Act of Parliament in 1874
Located next to the Bodleian Library on Catte Street
Famous for its 'Bridge of Sighs' architectural feature
Pioneer of state school access from the 1960s
Predecessor institutions include Hart Hall (c.1282) and Magdalen Hall

finances.bursaries: Oxford Bursary and college-specific support
finances.description: A well-supported college with particular commitment to access and widening participation.

oxford-jesus.name: Jesus College
oxford-jesus.overview: Jesus College was founded by Queen Elizabeth I in 1571, largely through the efforts of Hugh Price from Brecon, Wales. It has traditionally strong ties with Wales and remains one of the few Oxford colleges with a distinctive national character. The college has 340 undergraduates and 190 postgraduates.
oxford-jesus.character: Informally known as 'The Welsh College', Jesus has a warm, welcoming culture and takes pride in its Welsh heritage and international diversity. It is a mid-sized, friendly college with a strong sense of community.

accommodation.description: The college guarantees accommodation for all three years of undergraduate study, split between the main Turl Street site and Ship Street annexe.

notableAlumni: T. E. Lawrence (Lawrence of Arabia, Archaeologist and Military Officer)
Norman Washington Manley (Chief Minister of Jamaica)
keyFacts: Founded by Royal Charter in 1571 by Queen Elizabeth I
Historically known as 'The Welsh College'
First Oxford college to have Welsh connections at its foundation
340 undergraduates and 190 postgraduates
Strong tradition of public service and political engagement

finances.bursaries: Oxford Bursary and college access funds
finances.description: Good financial resources with a strong emphasis on supporting students from diverse backgrounds.

oxford-keble.name: Keble College
oxford-keble.overview: Keble College was founded in 1870 as a memorial to John Keble of the Oxford Movement, with the aim of making Oxford education accessible to students of modest means. It is one of Oxford's largest colleges, with 465 undergraduates and 550 postgraduates, and is famous for its striking Victorian red-brick Gothic architecture.
oxford-keble.character: Known as one of Oxford's most welcoming and inclusive colleges, Keble has a strong communal ethos rooted in its founding ideals. Its distinctive architecture and commitment to common table (regular shared dining) create a distinctive community feel.

accommodation.description: Keble offers accommodation to all undergraduates for all three years of their course, with 370 rooms on its large main site. From second year students can choose to live out.

keyFacts: Founded 1870 as a memorial to John Keble
One of Oxford's largest colleges with over 1,000 students
Famous for its distinctive red-brick Victorian Gothic architecture
Purpose-built with a large dining hall for common table dining
Strong tradition of inclusivity and access

finances.bursaries: Oxford Bursary and college-specific access support
finances.description: A well-resourced college with significant conference income supporting student facilities.

oxford-lady-margaret-hall.name: Lady Margaret Hall
oxford-lady-margaret-hall.overview: Lady Margaret Hall was founded in 1878 by a group of High Anglicans including Edward Talbot and his wife Lavinia, opening in October 1879 as one of Oxford's first two women's colleges under principal Elizabeth Wordsworth. The college has nearly 400 undergraduates and around 200 postgraduates, and sits in extensive grounds in North Oxford.
oxford-lady-margaret-hall.character: LMH has a distinctive liberal, high-achieving culture and was among the first Oxford colleges to admit women. Since going mixed in 1979, it has maintained a strong commitment to gender equality and diversity, producing an impressive roster of high-profile women alumni.

accommodation.description: The college provides accommodation for all undergraduates throughout their course in its large North Oxford gardens site, offering a range of room types in historic and modern buildings.

keyFacts: Founded 1878 as one of Oxford's first two women's colleges
Opened October 1879 with nine students
Admitted men in 1979
Located in extensive gardens in North Oxford
Notable for producing pioneering women leaders

finances.bursaries: Oxford Bursary and LMH access bursaries
finances.description: Good endowment strengthened by major alumni donations; committed to access and inclusion.

oxford-lincoln.name: Lincoln College
oxford-lincoln.overview: Lincoln College was founded in 1427 by Richard Fleming, Bishop of Lincoln, and is one of Oxford's smaller undergraduate colleges, with around 300 undergraduates and 300 postgraduates. It is noted for preserving more of the character of a 15th-century college than any other in Oxford, according to architectural historian Nikolaus Pevsner.
oxford-lincoln.character: A small, tight-knit college with an exceptionally strong community spirit. Lincoln's compact size means students quickly become close, and its Junior Common Room plays an unusually large role in college life. Known for academic quality alongside a sociable atmosphere.

accommodation.description: The college guarantees accommodation for all years of an undergraduate's course. First years live in main college with en-suite bathrooms; later years are housed in nearby central Oxford properties.

keyFacts: Founded 1427 by Bishop Richard Fleming
Preserves more 15th-century character than any other Oxford college
Small college of around 300 undergraduates
Located on Turl Street in central Oxford
John Wesley was a Fellow here in the 1720s

finances.bursaries: Oxford Bursary and Lincoln College bursaries
finances.description: Mid-sized endowment with strong commitment to student support.

oxford-magdalen.name: Magdalen College
oxford-magdalen.overview: Magdalen College was founded in 1458 by William Waynflete, Bishop of Winchester and Lord Chancellor. One of Oxford's wealthiest and most beautiful colleges, it is set in extensive grounds including a deer park, Water Meadow, and Addison's Walk along the River Cherwell. It is consistently one of Oxford's top-performing colleges academically.
oxford-magdalen.character: Magdalen combines academic excellence with extraordinary natural beauty, offering students an almost rural experience in the heart of the city. The college is known for its tradition of May Morning choir singing from the Great Tower, and for a balanced, high-achieving community.

accommodation.description: Students are housed across several residential quads on the large riverside site, including St Swithun's Quad, Longwall Quad, and the Grove Buildings. All accommodation is within the extensive college grounds.

keyFacts: Founded 1458 by Bishop William Waynflete
Extensive grounds include a deer park and riverside walk
10 Nobel Prize winners among alumni
Famous for May Morning singing from the Great Tower
One of Oxford's wealthiest colleges with endowment of ~£153m+

finances.bursaries: Oxford Bursary plus Magdalen scholarships and access awards
finances.description: One of Oxford's richest colleges, with strong endowment income supporting scholarships and student amenities.

oxford-mansfield.name: Mansfield College
oxford-mansfield.overview: Mansfield College was founded in Birmingham in 1838 as a college for Nonconformist students and moved to Oxford in 1886. It gained full university college status in 1995. The college has approximately 245 undergraduates, 145 postgraduates, and 40 visiting students, and holds one of the highest proportions of state-educated students at Oxford.
oxford-mansfield.character: Mansfield has the highest proportion of state-educated undergraduates of any Oxford college and is deeply committed to access and widening participation. It has a progressive ethos, an excellent central location, and one of the largest JCRs (common rooms) in Oxford.

accommodation.description: All undergraduates are guaranteed accommodation throughout their course. First years live on the main site in en-suite rooms; subsequent years are in college accommodation within easy cycling distance.

keyFacts: Full college status achieved in 1995
Highest proportion of state-educated students of any Oxford college
One quarter of UK undergraduates are first-generation university students
Founded for Nonconformist students in 1838, moved to Oxford 1886
Located close to the Science Area and university libraries

finances.bursaries: Oxford Bursary and Mansfield-specific access bursaries
finances.description: Smaller endowment compared to older colleges, but strong commitment to access and financial support for students.

oxford-merton.name: Merton College
oxford-merton.overview: Merton College was founded in 1264 by Walter de Merton, Lord Chancellor and Bishop of Rochester, and has a strong claim to be Oxford's oldest college, being the first to have a formal set of governing statutes. It has 315 undergraduates and 218 postgraduates, and its medieval buildings—including Mob Quad, the oldest quadrangle at any Oxford or Cambridge college—are among the finest in Oxford.
oxford-merton.character: Merton is known for its rigorous academic culture, intimate community, and extraordinary medieval setting. The college has a proud scholarly tradition and consistently performs well in the Norrington Table. It maintains a close-knit atmosphere with strong relationships between students and Fellows.

accommodation.description: The college offers accommodation for all three years of undergraduate study within or very close to the medieval main site on Merton Street.

keyFacts: Founded 1264, one of Oxford's three oldest colleges
Home to Mob Quad, the oldest college quadrangle in Oxford
5 Nobel Prize winners among alumni
J. R. R. Tolkien was Merton Professor of English here
Endowment of ~£337m (7th wealthiest Oxford college)

finances.bursaries: Oxford Bursary and Merton scholarships
finances.description: One of Oxford's wealthier colleges with an endowment of around £337m, supporting strong academic resources and student welfare.

oxford-new-college.name: New College
oxford-new-college.overview: New College was founded in 1379 by William of Wykeham, Bishop of Winchester, alongside Winchester College as its feeder school. Despite its name, it is one of Oxford's oldest colleges. The college has 426 undergraduates and 222 postgraduates, and is renowned for its medieval architecture, beautiful gardens, and Antechapel containing Epstein's Lazarus sculpture.
oxford-new-college.character: A large, architecturally magnificent college with a reputation for academic seriousness and institutional prestige. New College has a broad and distinguished alumni base spanning law, politics, media, and the arts, and retains a powerful sense of historical identity.

accommodation.description: The college offers accommodation within its large site on Holywell Street for all years of undergraduate study, including rooms in the historic medieval buildings and more modern accommodation.

keyFacts: Founded 1379 by Bishop William of Wykeham
Produced 7 Prime Ministers, 12 Archbishops, and 50 Nobel Laureates (alumni and fellows)
Famously still called 'New' despite being 645 years old
Features Epstein's Lazarus sculpture in the Antechapel
One of Oxford's larger colleges with 426 undergraduates

finances.bursaries: Oxford Bursary and New College scholarships
finances.description: Well-endowed college with strong financial support structures for students.

oxford-oriel.name: Oriel College
oxford-oriel.overview: Oriel College was founded in 1326, making it the fifth-oldest Oxford college, and was the first to be founded by a reigning monarch (Edward II). The college has approximately 300 undergraduates and 180 postgraduates, and is set around attractive quads near the Bodleian Library and Christ Church Meadow.
oxford-oriel.character: A small, close-knit college with a notably friendly atmosphere. Oriel was the last Oxford men's college to admit women (1985), but today has roughly equal numbers of men and women. The college is known for strong pastoral support and a welcoming multi-year community.

accommodation.description: All undergraduates are offered accommodation throughout their studies. The main site is supplemented by a residential annexe on Rectory Road, approximately ten minutes' walk away, largely for finalists and postgraduates.

notableAlumni: Cecil Rhodes (Mining Magnate and Founder of Rhodes Scholarship)
keyFacts: Founded 1326 as the fifth-oldest Oxford college
First Oxford college founded by a reigning monarch (Edward II)
Last men's Oxford college to admit women (1985)
Small, close-knit community of ~300 undergraduates
Cecil Rhodes studied here; Rhodes Scholarships named in his honour

finances.bursaries: Oxford Bursary and college access funds
finances.description: Mid-range endowment with strong commitment to student support.

oxford-pembroke.name: Pembroke College
oxford-pembroke.overview: Pembroke College was founded in 1624 by King James I and named after William Herbert, 3rd Earl of Pembroke. The college has 366 undergraduates and 179 postgraduates, and occupies a central site on St Aldate's with a mix of historic and modern buildings including the Rokos Quadrangle (2013).
oxford-pembroke.character: Pembroke is informally known as one of Oxford's 'fun' colleges, with an extremely sociable student body, strong sports culture, and a famous pink mascot theme. Despite this reputation for friendliness, the college is also academically strong.

accommodation.description: The college offers accommodation for all three years of undergraduate study, with rooms in both the 17th-century historic buildings and the modern Geoffrey Arthur Buildings and Rokos Quadrangle.

notableAlumni: J. William Fulbright (US Senator, founder of Fulbright Program)
keyFacts: Founded 1624 by King James I
J. R. R. Tolkien wrote The Hobbit and The Lord of the Rings here as a Fellow
Samuel Johnson attended but did not complete his degree
Endowment of ~£58.9m
Home to the Rokos Quadrangle (2013)

finances.bursaries: Oxford Bursary and Pembroke access scholarships
finances.description: Solid endowment with significant recent fundraising and new building development.

oxford-queens.name: The Queen's College
oxford-queens.overview: The Queen's College was founded in 1341 by Robert de Eglesfield in honour of Queen Philippa of Hainault, consort of Edward III. It has 333 undergraduates and 169 postgraduates, and is distinguished by its grand 18th-century neoclassical buildings on the High Street, designed in part by Nicholas Hawksmoor.
oxford-queens.character: Queen's retains a dignified, traditional atmosphere with strong academic standards. It is one of the few Oxford colleges to maintain a tabard-style uniform worn at formal dinners, and has a distinctive tradition of a trumpet fanfare before feasts.

accommodation.description: The college provides accommodation for all years, divided between the main High Street buildings, the Carrodus Quad (en-suite first-year rooms), and the nearby St Aldate's House annexe with 90 en-suite rooms.

notableAlumni: Tim Berners-Lee (Inventor of the World Wide Web)
keyFacts: Founded 1341 by Robert de Eglesfield
Grand neoclassical buildings on the High Street
Tim Berners-Lee invented the World Wide Web after studying here
Rowan Atkinson among famous alumni
Known for formal dining traditions and trumpet fanfares

finances.bursaries: Oxford Bursary and Queen's College support
finances.description: Well-established endowment supporting a historic college with extensive facilities.

oxford-st-annes.name: St Anne's College
oxford-st-annes.overview: St Anne's College was founded in 1879 as the Society of Oxford Home Students to allow women of limited means to study at Oxford, gaining full college status in 1959. One of Oxford's larger colleges, it has approximately 425 undergraduates and 291 postgraduates, situated on Woodstock Road in North Oxford.
oxford-st-annes.character: St Anne's has a strong commitment to diversity and access, rooted in its founding mission to allow women of any financial background to study at Oxford. The college is known for its open, inclusive atmosphere and modern outlook.

accommodation.description: The college accommodates undergraduates on the college site for all three years in a mixture of Victorian houses and purpose-built blocks. Graduate students have access to Robert Saunders House in Summertown and Eleanor Plumer House on the college campus.

keyFacts: Founded 1879 as the Society of Oxford Home Students
Full college status achieved in 1959
Located on Woodstock Road in North Oxford
One of Oxford's larger colleges with over 700 students
Founded with mission to allow women of all financial backgrounds to study at Oxford

finances.bursaries: Oxford Bursary and St Anne's access scholarships
finances.description: Good financial resources with strong emphasis on access in line with the college's founding mission.

oxford-st-catharines.name: St Catherine's College
oxford-st-catharines.overview: St Catherine's College achieved full college status in 1962, having grown from the Delegacy for Unattached Students founded in 1868. The college campus was designed entirely by Danish architect Arne Jacobsen and is a Grade I-listed masterpiece of modernist design. It has 494 undergraduates and 337 postgraduates, making it one of the largest Oxbridge colleges.
oxford-st-catharines.character: St Catherine's has a uniquely modern aesthetic and open, progressive culture. Its Jacobsen-designed buildings, grounds, and even furniture make it architecturally unlike any other Oxford college. It attracts students who value a less traditional Oxford experience.

accommodation.description: The college provides accommodation for all years of undergraduate study, primarily in the purpose-built Jacobsen buildings and more recent additions on the main Manor Road site.

notableAlumni: Eric Williams (Prime Minister of Trinidad and Tobago)
keyFacts: Full college status in 1962; roots in 1868 Delegacy
Entire campus designed by Arne Jacobsen in the 1960s
Buildings, furniture and gardens are Grade I-listed
One of the largest Oxford colleges with 831 students
Architectural historian Pevsner called it 'a perfect piece of architecture'

finances.bursaries: Oxford Bursary and college access support
finances.description: Well-resourced modern college with significant facilities on a purpose-designed campus.

oxford-st-edmund-hall.name: St Edmund Hall
oxford-st-edmund-hall.overview: St Edmund Hall — known as 'Teddy Hall' — is estimated to have been founded around 1236 and is the only surviving medieval academic hall in Oxford, predating the formal college system. It has approximately 400 undergraduates and 200 postgraduates, set around a picturesque small quadrangle in the heart of Oxford.
oxford-st-edmund-hall.character: Teddy Hall is known for its exceptionally strong sports culture and very high levels of student satisfaction alongside good academic performance. It has a particularly tight-knit, friendly community and an informally proud Aularian identity as the last surviving medieval hall.

accommodation.description: The college provides accommodation for all years of undergraduate study, in buildings on and near the compact main site on Queen's Lane.

keyFacts: The only surviving medieval academic hall at Oxford, c.1236
Known as 'Teddy Hall' by students
Strong sporting culture and high student satisfaction
Members are known as Aularians, not 'members of college'
Keir Starmer is a notable modern alumnus

finances.bursaries: Oxford Bursary and Teddy Hall support funds
finances.description: Mid-range endowment with strong emphasis on student community and welfare.

oxford-st-hildas.name: St Hilda's College
oxford-st-hildas.overview: St Hilda's College was founded in 1893 by Dorothea Beale as a women's college, and remained all-female until 2008 when it became the last Oxford college to go coeducational. The college has 410 undergraduates and 283 postgraduates, and is the only Oxford college lying east of the River Cherwell.
oxford-st-hildas.character: St Hilda's is an inclusive, friendly college with a beautiful riverside setting and a strong commitment to student wellbeing. Its late transition to coeducation has given it a distinctive identity, and it maintains a warm, accessible atmosphere.

accommodation.description: All first years live on the main college site and students may choose to live in college for all years of their degree. The college has a dining hall overlooking the river, offering sustainable, local and seasonal food.

notableAlumni: Zanny Minton Beddoes (First Female Editor-in-Chief, The Economist)
Zeinab Badawi (BBC Journalist, President of SOAS)
keyFacts: Founded 1893 by Dorothea Beale as a women's college
Last Oxford college to admit men (2008)
Only Oxford college east of the River Cherwell
Beautiful riverside site with gardens
Over 400 undergraduates and strong postgraduate community

finances.bursaries: Oxford Bursary and St Hilda's bursaries
finances.description: Good endowment supporting student welfare, with riverside facilities and strong community ethos.

oxford-st-hughs.name: St Hugh's College
oxford-st-hughs.overview: St Hugh's College was founded in 1886 by Elizabeth Wordsworth as a women's college to provide affordable Oxford education for women of limited means. The college admitted its first male students in 1986 in its centenary year. It has approximately 400 undergraduates and 400 postgraduates, set in 14 acres of beautiful gardens in North Oxford.
oxford-st-hughs.character: St Hugh's is known for its informal, welcoming atmosphere and extraordinary garden setting. The college is proud of its history as a women's college and actively strives to maintain a diverse, inclusive community. Gowns are rarely worn, and the relaxed tone is a distinctive feature.

accommodation.description: The college is large enough to accommodate all undergraduates throughout their degree in its extensive North Oxford site.

notableAlumni: Aung San Suu Kyi (Nobel Peace Prize Laureate, Politician)
Heather Hallett, Baroness Hallett (Chair of COVID-19 Inquiry)
keyFacts: Founded 1886 by Elizabeth Wordsworth
Went coeducational in 1986 (its centenary year)
14 acres of gardens — among the largest of any Oxford college
Known as 'the island site' for its secluded garden setting
Notable for producing high-profile women in law and politics

finances.bursaries: Oxford Bursary and St Hugh's hardship/access funds
finances.description: Good resources supporting a large college with extensive grounds and facilities.

oxford-st-johns.name: St John's College
oxford-st-johns.overview: St John's College was founded in 1555 by Sir Thomas White, a merchant and Lord Mayor of London, and is the wealthiest Oxford college with assets over £790 million as of 2022. It has 419 undergraduates and 244 postgraduates, situated on St Giles' in central Oxford.
oxford-st-johns.character: St John's is renowned for exceptional academic standards, unmatched financial resources, and a highly supportive environment. Its wealth means undergraduates benefit from some of the best-funded college facilities and financial support at Oxford.

accommodation.description: St John's is able to accommodate all undergraduates for the entirety of their course, including fourth years. Its substantial wealth enables it to offer heavily subsidised accommodation and meals.

notableAlumni: Lester B. Pearson (Canadian Prime Minister, Nobel Peace Prize)
keyFacts: Founded 1555 by Sir Thomas White
Oxford's wealthiest college with assets over £790m
Guarantees accommodation for all four years of undergraduate study
Tony Blair studied PPE here
Heavily subsidised food and accommodation due to large endowment

finances.bursaries: Exceptionally generous bursaries and scholarships; among the most supportive in Oxford
finances.description: By far the wealthiest Oxford college, with huge endowment income enabling heavily subsidised student costs and extensive support.

oxford-st-peters.name: St Peter's College
oxford-st-peters.location: New Inn Hall Street, central Oxford
oxford-st-peters.overview: St Peter's College was founded in 1929 as St Peter's Hall by Francis Chavasse, former Bishop of Liverpool, with the explicit aim of providing an Oxford education for students deterred by costs. It achieved full collegiate status in 1961. The college has 353 undergraduates and 224 postgraduates, on a site incorporating medieval buildings on New Inn Hall Street.
oxford-st-peters.character: St Peter's is known for its strong commitment to diversity and inclusion, informal atmosphere, and excellent relations between students and tutors. It was unusual among Oxford colleges in having Black undergraduates from its very foundation in 1929, and maintains a progressive, welcoming ethos.

accommodation.description: The college provides accommodation for all three years of undergraduate study, with rooms on the historic main site on New Inn Hall Street and in college-owned properties nearby.

notableAlumni: Carl Albert (Speaker of the US House of Representatives)
keyFacts: Founded 1929 as St Peter's Hall by Bishop Chavasse
Full collegiate status achieved 1961
Founded with mission to make Oxford accessible regardless of cost
Had Black undergraduates from its very foundation
Strong commitment to diversity and inclusion

finances.bursaries: Oxford Bursary and St Peter's access support
finances.description: Smaller endowment than older colleges, but deeply committed to access and student financial support.

oxford-somerville.name: Somerville College
oxford-somerville.overview: Somerville College was founded in 1879 as one of Oxford's first two women's colleges, with a nondenominational ethos to distinguish it from the High Anglican Lady Margaret Hall. It began admitting men in 1994. The college has 408 undergraduates and 207 postgraduates, and is one of only three Oxford colleges that can house all its undergraduates on the main site for all years.
oxford-somerville.character: Somerville has a proud tradition of producing trailblazing women in public life, and retains a liberal, egalitarian culture. It is known for its strong academics, welcoming community, and relaxed tone — gowns are not worn at Formal Hall.

accommodation.description: One of only three Oxford colleges that provides on-site accommodation for all undergraduates throughout their entire degree. The Dorothy Hodgkin Quad features self-contained flats with communal kitchens.

notableAlumni: Dorothy Hodgkin (Nobel Prize, Chemistry — only British woman to win a science Nobel)
keyFacts: Founded 1879 as one of Oxford's first two women's colleges
Nondenominational from the outset
Admitted men from 1994
Produced two prime ministers: Thatcher and Gandhi
Endowment of over £91m

finances.bursaries: Oxford Bursary and Somerville access scholarships
finances.description: Growing endowment (now over £91m) supporting a well-resourced college with strong access commitments.

oxford-trinity.name: Trinity College
oxford-trinity.overview: Trinity College was founded in 1555 by Sir Thomas Pope on land previously occupied by Durham College. One of Oxford's larger historic colleges in terms of buildings, it has approximately 400 undergraduates and sits on extensive grounds in the heart of Oxford between Balliol and the Sheldonian Theatre.
oxford-trinity.character: Trinity has a reputation for academic quality and a traditional, lively social culture. It has produced three British prime ministers, and its gardens and grounds give it a surprisingly spacious feel in the centre of the city.

accommodation.description: The college provides accommodation for all three years of undergraduate study, primarily within or adjacent to the main Broad Street site and its substantial gardens.

notableAlumni: Cardinal Reginald Pole (Last Catholic Archbishop of Canterbury)
keyFacts: Founded 1555 by Sir Thomas Pope
Produced 3 British Prime Ministers
Endowment of ~£224m (£191m financial endowment)
Located between Balliol and the Sheldonian on Broad Street
Extensive gardens for a central Oxford college

finances.bursaries: Oxford Bursary and Trinity scholarships
finances.description: Well-endowed college with total funds exceeding £224m, supporting strong student facilities.

oxford-university-college.name: University College
oxford-university-college.overview: University College — known as 'Univ' — was founded in 1249 by William of Durham and has a strong claim to be Oxford's oldest college. It has 401 undergraduates and 223 postgraduates, located on the High Street in central Oxford. The college expelled Percy Bysshe Shelley in 1811 for publishing a pamphlet on atheism.
oxford-university-college.character: Univ has a serious academic reputation, a central location, and a friendly, well-rounded student community. Its alumni span politics, literature, science, and media, and the college takes pride in intellectual diversity alongside high academic standards.

accommodation.description: The college aims to accommodate all undergraduates for their entire course. It has over 400 study bedrooms, with more than 300 on the main High Street site, plus an annexe in North Oxford on Staverton Road and some rooms on Iffley Road.

keyFacts: Founded 1249 by William of Durham — Oxford's oldest college
Known as 'Univ' by students
Expelled Percy Bysshe Shelley in 1811
Endowment of ~£255m total assets
Bill Clinton and Stephen Hawking both attended

finances.bursaries: Oxford Bursary and Univ scholarships and hardship funds
finances.description: Strong endowment supporting a well-resourced central Oxford college.

oxford-wadham.name: Wadham College
oxford-wadham.overview: Wadham College was founded in 1610 by Dorothy Wadham in accordance with the will of her late husband Nicholas Wadham, and is one of Oxford's largest colleges with about 480 undergraduates and 240 postgraduates. The college is noted for its scientific heritage: the Oxford Philosophical Club, precursor to the Royal Society, met at Wadham in the 1650s.
oxford-wadham.character: Wadham is consistently regarded as one of Oxford's most liberal and progressive colleges. It was among the first to admit women (1974), flies the rainbow flag during Queer Week, and is known for its activist, inclusive, and informal culture. The college attracts students who want genuine intellectual and social engagement.

accommodation.description: The college provides accommodation for all three years of undergraduate study on its large central site on Parks Road, with various room types and shared facilities.

keyFacts: Founded 1610 by Dorothy Wadham
Precursor to the Royal Society met at Wadham in the 1650s
One of the first Oxford colleges to admit women (1974)
Known for liberal, progressive culture and strong LGBTQ+ support
One of Oxford's largest colleges with ~720 students

finances.bursaries: Oxford Bursary and Wadham access support
finances.description: Well-resourced college with strong commitment to access and a large, active student community.

oxford-worcester.name: Worcester College
oxford-worcester.overview: Worcester College was founded in 1714 through the benefaction of Sir Thomas Cookes of Worcestershire, on a site where Gloucester College—a monastic institution—had stood since the 13th century. The college has 411 undergraduates and 187 postgraduates, and is set in 26 acres of award-winning gardens including a private lake.
oxford-worcester.character: Worcester is known for its happy, supportive community, stunning grounds, and architecturally diverse buildings spanning medieval to Georgian. It attracts students who value excellent facilities, an oasis of green space in central Oxford, and a strong but unpretentious academic culture.

accommodation.description: Worcester provides accommodation for all undergraduates for three years, with all rooms on the main site or within 300 metres of the perimeter. Over two-thirds of bedrooms are en-suite. Buildings include the historic Gloucester House and the modern Earl and Linbury Buildings.

notableAlumni: Bill Bradley (US Senator and Basketball Player)
keyFacts: Founded 1714 by benefaction of Sir Thomas Cookes
26 acres of gardens including a private lake
Medieval buildings alongside 18th-century neoclassical architecture
All accommodation within 300m of the main site
Endowment of ~£60m

finances.bursaries: Oxford Bursary and Worcester College support funds
finances.description: Solid endowment of ~£60m supporting a well-maintained college with outstanding grounds and facilities.
